steps.title: Identify what the blank must do grammatically
steps.content:
The blank follows the noun “inventions” and introduces a list of examples. A participial phrase using a present participle (“including”) is the correct structure to supplement a main clause with illustrative examples. A finite verb would create a structural error because the sentence already has a main verb (“patented”). Evaluate each choice:
  • Choice A — ‘included’: a finite past-tense verb — creates a second main clause without a conjunction, producing a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘includes’: a finite present-tense verb — same structural problem; tense also conflicts with the past-tense context. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘including’: a nonfinite present participle — correctly forms a participial phrase that supplements the main clause without creating a run-on. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘will include’: a finite future-tense verb — same structural problem; future tense is illogical for inventions already patented. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: Only “including” is a nonfinite participle that can legally supplement the main clause “he patented many inventions” by listing examples. That matches choice C — C is correct.
